title: Coverage of essential health services
subtitle: Coverage of essential health services is measured as an index on a scale of 0 to 100 (where higher is better). This index is based on risk-standardized death rates – which give a measure of healthcare access and quality – and whether common interventions are carried out.
caption:
Coverage of essential health services

Coverage of essential health services is measured as an index on a scale of 0 to 100 (where higher is better). This index is based on risk-standardized death rates – which give a measure of healthcare access and quality – and whether common interventions are carried out.

The choropleth map displays the 2023 coverage of essential health services across the globe, utilizing a continuous color scale ranging from pale green for the lowest scores to deep blue for the highest scores on an index of 0 to 100. This metric aggregates data on risk-standardized death rates and the implementation of common interventions to evaluate healthcare access and quality, encompassing services for health promotion, prevention, treatment, and care. The highest values, represented by the darkest blue shades indicating scores approaching 100, are visible across North America, Western and Northern Europe, Australia, and parts of East Asia including Japan and South Korea. In South America, countries such as Brazil and Argentina also appear in the darker blue bands, signifying high coverage relative to the global distribution. A middle range of coverage, shown in medium blue and teal tones corresponding to index values roughly between 60 and 80, characterizes much of Eastern Europe, Russia, China, and Mexico. Lower scores, depicted in lighter green and pale blue shades falling between 20 and 60, are concentrated across the majority of the African continent and parts of South Asia, including India and Pakistan. Greenland and a few other territories are marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no data is available. The geographic distribution shows a clear concentration of the highest index scores in high-income regions of the Northern Hemisphere and Oceania, while the lowest scores cluster in sub-Saharan Africa and specific areas of Asia.
